frameworks.name: information theoretic encoding capacity
frameworks.one_line: Sensory encoding accuracy quantified as bounded information capacity from psychophysics.

frameworks.name: sensory-prior weighting imbalance account of ASD
frameworks.one_line: Existing view that ASD reflects altered prior/likelihood balance in inference.

frameworks.name: efficient coding / adaptation to stimulus statistics
frameworks.one_line: Neural representations should track environmental orientation statistics.

core_question: Do individuals with ASD differ from neurotypicals in sensory encoding capacity and its adaptation to changing stimulus statistics?
key_assumptions: Encoding accuracy is recoverable from psychophysical performance via an information-theoretic measure
Neurotypical sensory representations initially reflect natural-scene orientation statistics
Feedback under altered statistics can reshape sensory encoding
manipulations: Stimulus distribution: natural-scene orientation prior vs uniform artificial distribution
Performance feedback during exposure to the artificial distribution
key_findings: Initial encoding in both groups reflected natural-scene orientation statistics but capacity was lower in ASD
Neurotypicals shifted sensory representations toward the new uniform statistics and increased total encoding capacity
ASD group showed no significant change in total capacity or its allocation
Degree of adaptation correlated with initial encoding capacity across both groups
interpretations: ASD involves not only altered prior/likelihood weighting but also reduced and less adaptive sensory encoding capacity
Adaptive reallocation of encoding resources to current stimulus statistics is impaired in ASD
